description:
## The change

Today the typer has four loops where one belongs:

```
type_module:
  for _ in 0..16 {                                   ← outer LFP
    type_module_pass:
      phase-1 worklist (the only sane one)
      for _ in 0..64 {                               ← phase-2 outer
        snapshot specs; sweep MakeClosure
        for _ in 0..16 {                             ← phase-2 inner
          snapshot specs; re-walk all of them
        }
      }
    compute_effective_returns:
      for _ in 0..(N*4 + 16) {                       ← returns LFP
        rebuild all returns from scratch
      }
  }
```

Three of the four rebuild whole-graph state from scratch each pass.
Phase 1's worklist is the only one that scales.

Replace all four with one worklist driven by two trigger conditions:

1. **A spec is newly registered** → walk it once.
2. **A spec's effective_return changed** → revisit specs that read
   it as a callsite return or cont slot-0.

The reverse index makes condition (2) O(changed-edges) instead of
O(all-specs · iter-count).

## Shape

```rust
// (callee_key) → [caller_keys that read its effective_return]
callsite_index: HashMap<(FnId, Vec<Descr>), Vec<(FnId, Vec<Descr>)>>

// (FnId, Vec<Descr>) entries needing (re-)walk
worklist: VecDeque<(FnId, Vec<Descr>)>
```

Per worklist step: type_fn the spec, update effective_returns, emit
discovered callsites + cont sites as pending, drain pending into
specs + worklist, and propagate return-changes via callsite_index.

Phase-1 / phase-2 collapse into one queue — MakeClosure-driven any-key
registration and direct-callsite discovery are both forms of
"spec X discovers spec Y is reachable."

## Why this terminates

Same argument the current code already relies on: effective_returns
grow monotonically under union; the lattice is finite (bounded by
program-visible types). Each spec's return can change at most
lattice-height times. Worklist drains.

## Removals

- `type_module`'s `for _ in 0..16` outer LFP loop (`ir_typer.rs:477-490`).
- The phase-1 / phase-2 split inside `type_module_pass`.
- `for _ in 0..64` outer phase-2 loop (`ir_typer.rs:695`).
- `for _ in 0..16` inner phase-2 re-walk (`ir_typer.rs:742`).
- Both `let snapshot = specs.keys().cloned().collect();` clones.
- `compute_effective_returns`'s `for _ in 0..(specs.len() * 4 + 16)`
  loop (`ir_typer.rs:2444`). `compute_effective_returns` becomes a
  lookup against the worklist's incrementally-maintained map, or
  goes away entirely if that map is the canonical store.
- The `prev_returns: HashMap<...>` outer-iter cache —
  effective_returns is just live state under the worklist invariant.

## What stays

- `type_fn` (per-spec body typing) — unchanged. Its own inner
  fixpoint over block envs becomes much cheaper once TYP.1 lands
  but the algorithm is unchanged.
- `walk_spec_for_discovery` — unchanged.
- `Descr` operations — unchanged.
- Spec keying by `(FnId, Vec<Descr>)` — unchanged.
- Entry seeding from `entry_seeds(m)` — unchanged.
- SCC widening at `WIDEN_AT` for recursive list-walk termination —
  preserved (worklist tracks per-spec iter count for its SCC).

## Acceptance

- One worklist; no `for _ in 0..N` whole-graph rebuilds in the typer.
- All existing typer + codegen tests pass unchanged.
- `ast_eval` per-call `type_module` cost drops from ~25 ms
  (post-TYP.1) to under 10 ms — convergence in ~spec-count
  worklist steps instead of 11 outer iters × 58 specs each.
- Three-path parity preserved.

## Out of scope

- Switching spec keys from `Vec<Descr>` to `SpecId` — separate question.
- Canonicalizing `Descr` to make structural eq more often match
  semantic eq — separate question (TYP.1 already handles the hit).
- Caching `type_module` results across pipeline calls — TYP.2
  already eliminates the redundant call; the remaining two are
  genuinely different inputs.

## Risk

The biggest of the three. Self-mitigation: the algorithm is
standard data-flow LFP via worklist — taught in every compiler
course; well-understood; well-tested by the existing fixture
matrix once it lands.

## Independent of

TYP.1 and TYP.2. Lands last because the diff is largest and the
others give us cleaner baseline numbers.
title: fz-TYP.3: Replace nested typer fixpoints with one worklist
